problems.question: Explain the general principle of the Transform and Conquer design strategy. How does transforming a problem, such as through presorting, simplify operations like Searching, Duplicate Detection, and Median Finding?
problems.answer: Transform and Conquer solves a problem by transforming it into a simpler or more convenient representation. Presorting turns searching into O(log n) binary search, duplicate detection into a single O(n) adjacent pass, and median finding into O(1) index access, leading to a total complexity dominated by the O(n log n) sorting phase.

explanation.text: Instance Simplification: Transform the input into a simpler form (e.g., sort the array first).
Representation Change: Transform the problem representation into a different structure (e.g. Binary Search Tree or Heap).
Problem Reduction: Transform the problem into an instance of another problem for which an efficient algorithm is already known.

interactiveDesign.interactiveGoal: Help students understand why transforming a problem makes it easier to solve, and demonstrate how sorting simplifies tasks like Searching, Duplicate Detection, and Median Finding.
userInputs: Select problem type (Searching, Duplicate Detection, Scheduling)

dynamicVisualBehavior.layout: Split-screen interface displaying original messy representation on the left vs simplified transformed representation on the right.
dynamicVisualBehavior.execution: Students compare direct complexity O(n) or O(n^2) against transformed complexity O(n log n). Live complexity charts and comparison counters update in real time.

problems.question: Trace the Element Uniqueness problem using (a) Brute Force pairwise comparisons and (b) Transform-and-Conquer using presorting followed by adjacent element checking.
problems.answer: (a) Brute Force compares all pairs, requiring O(n^2) operations. (b) Transform-and-Conquer sorts the array first, then scans adjacent elements for duplicates, requiring O(n log n) sorting + O(n) checking, which equals O(n log n) overall.

explanation.text: Brute Force: Use nested loops to compare array[i] with array[j] for all i != j.
Transform: Sort the array using an O(n log n) sort like mergesort or heapsort.
Conquer: Scan adjacent elements (array[i] == array[i+1]) in a single loop. If any match is found, elements are not unique.

interactiveDesign.interactiveGoal: Visualize why pairwise comparison becomes expensive in brute force, and how sorting reduces duplicate detection to a single neighbor check sweep.

dynamicVisualBehavior.layout: Displays comparison matrix view for brute force vs sequential neighbor pointers in sorted sweep.
dynamicVisualBehavior.execution: In brute force mode, loops highlight cells in a comparison matrix. In sorting mode, pointer animations walk through sorted elements, alerting when adjacent duplicates match. Complexity dashboard compares operations in real-time.

problems.question: Compare search, insertion, and deletion efficiency of an unsorted Array, a Linked List, and a Binary Search Tree (BST). Explain why sequential insertions degrade a BST and how balancing corrects this.
problems.answer: Arrays and Linked Lists have O(n) search times. A balanced BST has O(log n) search. Sequential insertions in a BST (e.g. 1, 2, 3...) degenerate the tree into a linear list with O(n) height. Balancing keeps the tree height logarithmic, preserving O(log n) efficiency.

explanation.text: Unsorted Array / Linked List: Linear search scans elements one by one, giving O(n) complexity.
BST: Node selection divides the search space in half at each step (left for smaller, right for larger).
Degeneration: Inserting pre-sorted keys creates a single-branch tree (skewed), leading to worst-case O(n) height. Balancing maintains height <= 1.44 log2 n.

interactiveDesign.topic: BST vs Array vs Linked List & Unbalanced BST Problem
interactiveDesign.interactiveGoal: Compare operation costs across data structures and understand BST height degeneration under sequential inputs.
userInputs: Insert sequence selector (Worst case ascending vs Random)

dynamicVisualBehavior.layout: Three side-by-side structures (Array, Linked List, BST) perform the same operation simultaneously.
dynamicVisualBehavior.execution: ProgressBar speeds and operation counters update live. Height meter graph rises dramatically under worst-case sequential insertions to show degeneration.

problems.question: Construct an AVL Tree by inserting nodes. Trace height, check Balance Factors, and show LL, RR, LR, and RL rotations to restore balance.
problems.answer: An AVL tree is a self-balancing BST where the Balance Factor (BF = height(L) - height(R)) of every node is in {-1, 0, 1}. Any insertion causing BF = +2 or -2 triggers rotations: Single (LL, RR) or Double (LR, RL) to restore balance.

explanation.text: Insert the node as in a normal BST.
Compute Balance Factor = height(Left subtree) - height(Right subtree) for ancestor nodes.
If BF is +2 or -2, determine rotation type based on inserted node path: LL (Right rotation), RR (Left rotation), LR (Left-Right rotation), RL (Right-Left rotation).

interactiveDesign.interactiveGoal: Learn self-balancing behavior, balance factor computation (Height(Left) - Height(Right)), and single/double rotations.

dynamicVisualBehavior.layout: AVL tree structure displaying heights and balance factors beside nodes.
dynamicVisualBehavior.execution: Node selection highlights left/right subtrees and prints equation step-by-step. Triggering imbalance highlights 'Rebalance Required' and animates single/double node rotation shifts.

problems.question: Represent a Max-Heap as an array. Connect tree node positions with array indices, explain index formulas, and run the heap property audit.
problems.answer: A Max-Heap is a complete binary tree where parent >= children. It is stored as an array where for a 1-indexed node i: Parent = i/2, Left Child = 2i, Right Child = 2i+1. Max-Heap property is violated if parent < children.

explanation.text: Map tree nodes level-by-level (left to right) to array positions.
Compute parent-child index bounds: parent of index i is floor(i/2), left child is 2*i, right child is 2*i+1 (adjusting for 0-indexing: parent = (i-1)/2, left = 2i+1, right = 2i+2).
Verify the Max-Heap property: Array[parent] >= Array[child] for all valid indices.

interactiveDesign.interactiveGoal: Connect tree nodes with array positions, understand index formulas, and audit parent-child value properties.
userInputs: Click node or array index to highlight pair

dynamicVisualBehavior.layout: Binary tree node layout linked to a horizontal array layout.
dynamicVisualBehavior.execution: Selecting node i displays index formula and highlights parent/child cells. Violations of shape or max-heap property highlight nodes in red, alerting students.

problems.question: Step through the Heapsort process using bottom-up heapify, max key deletions, and sift-down operations. Verify its complexity.
problems.answer: Heapsort has two phases: (1) Build Heap in O(n) using bottom-up heapify starting from the last parent node down to the root. (2) Sort by swapping root (max) with the last element, decrementing heap size, and running sift-down on root in O(log n), repeating n-1 times. Total complexity is O(n log n).

explanation.text: Build heap: heapify parent nodes floor(n/2) down to 1. Sift-down swaps larger children until heap property is restored.
Swap root (index 0) with the last element of the active heap, locking the max element in sorted position.
Sift-down the root element on the remaining active heap bounds. Repeat until sorted region grows from right to left.

interactiveDesign.topic: Heap Sort Construction, Deletion, and Complexity
interactiveDesign.interactiveGoal: Visualize the full sorting process using bottom-up heapify sift-down passes, delete-max steps, and verify complexity curves.

dynamicVisualBehavior.layout: Four synchronized panels displaying array state, heap tree, pseudocode, and complexity tracker.
dynamicVisualBehavior.execution: Animations show root swapping with leaf, removing node from heap boundary, and sift-down bubble. Complexity tracker plots actual comparisons and swaps against n log n.
